entry.title: Why Collaborative Maintenance?
entry.content:
In today's world, many companies are moving from single-tier to multi-tier supply chains. While this shift provides flexibility, cost advantages, and resilience, it also introduces challenges, particularly around connectivity and visibility across suppliers, manufacturers, and service contractors.

  • Data Silos & Inconsistent Standards: Different systems among suppliers complicate data exchange and hinder interoperability. This can lead to significant delays and miscommunications.
  • Lack of Real-Time Visibility: Companies struggle to track assets and maintenance across tiers, resulting in potential disruptions and inefficiencies in the supply chain.
  • Business Partner Onboarding & Integration: Connecting new suppliers is complex, especially for smaller ones with limited technology infrastructure. This complexity can slow down the integration process and affect overall productivity.
  • Collaboration & Communication Gaps: Misalignment between tiers leads to errors and inefficiencies. Effective communication and collaboration are crucial to ensure smooth operations and minimize misunderstandings.

 

How to Overcome These Challenges?

 

SAP Business Network addresses multi-tier maintenance connectivity challenges by offering a collaborative platform for asset operators, OEMs, spare parts suppliers, and service contractors. This innovative platform streamlines maintenance collaboration across various supply chain tiers, enhancing efficiency and reducing potential disruptions.

 

 A multi-tier maintenance network creates value for both asset operators and service contractors by improving visibility, collaboration, and efficiency in maintenance operations. Here’s a breakdown of the value proposition from both perspectives:
 
Asset Operators
  • Automatically share work orders with qualified service contractors to ensure quick response times by connecting with multiple contractors and collaborating on a single source of truth. This approach streamlines communication, reduces delays, and ensures that all parties have access to the same accurate information.
  • Reduce maintenance data management costs by improving the efficiency of data handover processes.
  • Track the exact progress of a maintenance job in real time. Updates include job start time, in-progress status, completion, and documents.
Service Contractors
  • Expand your market reach by connecting with multiple asset operators on a single platform, enhancing your business opportunities and growth potential.
  • Improve wrench time by providing centralized access to all maintenance information, ensuring continuous collaboration among asset ecosystem partners and enhancing overall efficiency.
  • Enhance maintenance practices with accurate and comprehensive technical data to ensure efficiency and accuracy in all maintenance operations.

 Conclusion: Why Use SAP Business Network for Maintenance?

  • Improve asset data accuracy & completeness: Remove the data silos and collaborating on a common model taxonomy and create a consistent understanding.
  • Improve wrench time: Provide centralized access to all maintenance information with continuous collaboration among asset ecosystem partners.
  • Reduce maintenance data management costs: Improve efficiency of data handover from asset owners to service providers and reduce data maintenance and validation.
  • Reduce un-planned downtime or outages: Enable effective collaboration between the operator and service provider on equipment failures and maintenance performance.
  • Improve customer satisfaction: Manage of the full customer lifecycle from maintenance planning, scheduling, execution, and customer service.

 

Thanks for reading the blog post and Stay tuned!

We encourage you to visit the SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration Customer Influence Portal to submit your innovation request...

We hope you enjoy these new capabilities. Thanks for reading the blog post and Stay tuned!

Follow me on LinkedIn

entry.title: The Looming Tariff Tsunami: Is Your Business Ready?
entry.content:
“Tariff” is fast becoming the word of the year.

And the treat of tariffs is for sure the most talked about topic in boardrooms all over the world as they become “the hammer” in many geopolitical conversations!

Why?

Because over the past decades we have built often complex supply chains that span continents, delivering goods with incredible speed and competitive prices. However, this complexity also introduces vulnerabilities, and recently, tariffs have emerged as a major disruptor.

The Ripple Effect of Tariffs

When tariffs hit, the impact reverberates throughout the entire supply chain. To make any profit at all, importers need to pass some (if not all) of the increased costs onto customers, often leading to reduced demand. Retailers are then forced to rethink sourcing strategies, re-evaluate supplier relationships, and overhaul inventory management practices. The numbers can be staggering, with some estimates suggesting potential annual cost increases of thousands of dollars for consumers.

Adding to the challenge, businesses are already facing rising administrative costs (which have risen 14% from 2023-2024), regulatory pressures and the looming uncertainty of future trade policies. Leaders are actively seeking ways to mitigate these overhead expenses. While options like nearshoring or exploring new sourcing routes might seem appealing, they often prove to be short-term fixes with their own set of risks.

Beyond Reactive Measures: Embracing Technology-Driven Resilience

Instead of simply reacting to tariff changes, companies need a more proactive, long-term strategy. This is where technology, particularly advanced supply chain management solutions, comes into play. By harnessing the power of end-to-end data visibility, artificial intelligence (AI), and automation, businesses can transform their supply chains into resilient assets. They can not only withstand disruptions but emerge stronger and more adaptable:

  • Scenario Planning: Model "what-if" scenarios to test different supply chain strategies before implementation. By developing contingency plans to address potential disruptions through alternate sourcing regions, logistics routes, and pricing models you can minimize tariff exposure.
  • Alternative Sourcing Strategies: Because tariffs depend on country of origin, many companies will seek alternative suppliers in regions not impacted. In fact, nearly one-third of businesses are pursuing a dual supply chain approach where they have both global and local options. Business Network collaboration platforms not only help identify potential suppliers at risk but also provide alternative sources of supply and collaborative tools to ensure rapid and precise management of disruptions.
  • Inventory Optimization: Identifying key products that can be purchased in advance of tariffs to bolster inventory and ensuring that they are positioned in key locations across the supply chain will help minimize the impact of looming tariffs. Studies show that top-performing supply chain organizations are investing in AI and machine learning at twice the rate of their lower-performing peers.
  • Enhanced Supply Chain Visibility: Real-time tracking of demand fluctuations, inventory levels, and logistical operations, allowing businesses to swiftly adjust their strategies in response to new tariffs or other disruptions. This allows you to diversify suppliers, optimize inventory placement, and adjust logistics to minimize the impact of tariffs.

A Smarter Path Forward

In this volatile trade environment, resilience, agility, and adaptability are no longer optional – they're essential. By embracing the right technology, businesses can maintain competitiveness and ensure that tariffs don't dictate their success.

Discover how SAP Business Suite can help your business be more resilient to business challenges and responsive to opportunities.

Follow me on Twitter.

entry.title: Aluminum And Steel Tariffs: What Do You Mean My Beer Will Cost More?
entry.content:
I was watching the local news when I heard a report that hit me to the core.

A local microbrewer was explaining that they were being forced to raise the price of my favorite beer by $2 a case.

That piqued my interest, to say the least. Upon listening further, it became apparently clear the reason for the price hike was due to the rising cost of aluminum cans.

Again, I was glued to the TV to find out why.

The aluminum and steel tariffs are set to come into effect on March 12 (at 12:01 a.m. Eastern Standard Time to be precise). The tariffs will impose a 25% duty on all imports of aluminum and steel into the USA.

And that has a knock-on effect to my favorite libation! Apparently, the cost of the can I was now gently squeezing in my fist is 65% of the costs associated with my beverage of choice.

That’s 65% x 25% of a can of beer.

Talk about a sobering moment!

What are the downstream impacts?

But it is not only me that will be crying into my drink. The effects of these tariffs are significant and wide ranging.

On the beer can half empty side:

  • The Automotive Industry is estimating the cost of a new car could increase by between $4,000 – $12,000, which will in turn increase both the demand and price of used car prices as buyers seek cheaper alternatives.
  • Manufacturing sectors like industrial equipment manufacturers and household appliance producers are some of the hardest-hit industries, as they rely heavily on imported steel and aluminum. The tariffs increase production costs for these manufacturers, potentially leading to higher prices for consumers and decreased employment in these sectors.
  • The construction industry, which employs over 10 million Americans, is significantly impacted as increased steel cost make building projects more expensive and will potentially slow down construction activity and impact overall demand.
  • Consumer prices are expected to rise: As higher manufacturing costs are likely to be passed on to consumers, resulting in price increases for various products, including vehicles, canned products (including my beer), home appliances and construction materials.

But on the beer can half full side:

  • Recycling sector: In the short term, the tariffs may benefit the recycling industry by increasing demand for domestic sources of these metals, such as used beverage cans and containers.
  • Domestic metal industries: In the longer term, the goal is for U.S. steel and aluminum producers to benefit from reduced foreign competition, potentially leading to increased domestic production and job growth in these sectors.

These knock-on effects highlight the complex and far-reaching consequences of the aluminum and steel tariffs across various sectors of the economy and international trade relationships. Only time will tell!

Discover how SAP Business Suite can help your business be more resilient to business challenges and responsive to opportunities.

Follow me on Twitter.

entry.title: Introducing Data Extraction for PP/DS for SAP S/4HANA
entry.content:
Background

SAP S/4HANA Manufacturing for planning and scheduling (PP/DS) capability is used to create procurement proposals for in-house production or external procurement to cover product requirements and to optimize and plan the resource schedule and the order dates/times in detail. With the help of PP/DS, a Production Planner can reduce the lead times, increase on-time delivery performance, or increase the throughput of products and reduce the stock costs.

However, how can the Production Planners ensure that their production plans account for past trends, seasonality, and recurring bottlenecks, to drive more proactive decision-making?

In today's data-driven world, having access to accurate and timely data is crucial for making informed decisions. Depending on the complexity of the business, a production planner could be dealing with hundreds of resources, thousands of products, and thousands of partners and customers. The ability to extract production planning and scheduling data - and analyze it to uncover actionable insights - is critical for adapting future production plans and maintaining a competitive edge.

 

Introduction

As a part of the SAP S/4HANA 2023 FPS03, four Data Extraction CDS views have been released for PP/DS:

  • Order Details
  • Order Operations
  • Pegging Relationships
  • Resource Capacity

Using the above data extraction views, you can fetch PP/DS data from your SAP S/4HANA system and load it into your data warehouse for reporting, auditing, analytics, or record keeping.

 

Data Extraction Views

Each of the four Data Extraction CDS views given below have the following inputs:

  • Planning Version
  • Start Date
  • End Date
  • Work Area
  • Customizing ID (of the Work Area)

The Start Date and End Date are both inclusive, and the results are always returned in the UTC Time Zone. If your Work Area does not have a Customizing ID, add a single space in the Customizing ID field.

Order Details

The Data Extraction CDS view C_APSOrdDetsByWorkAreaDEX (Data Extraction view for Order Details by Work Area) extracts the order details including the status, duration, quantity, date, and time.

Order Operations

The Data Extraction CDS view C_APSOrdOpsByWorkAreaDEX (Data Extraction view for Order Operations by Work Area) extracts the order operation (and phases/suboperations) details including the status, duration, quantity, date, and time.

Pegging Relationships

The Data Extraction CDS view C_APSOrdToDmndByWorkAreaDEX (Data Extraction view for Pegging Relationships by Work Area) extracts the pegging relationship details between receipts and requirements including the status, quantity, date, and time.

Resource Capacity

The Data Extraction CDS view C_APSRsceCapByWorkAreaDEX (Data Extraction view for Resource Capacity by Work Area) extracts the capacity duration details for resources on every calendar day from the start date to the end date. The capacity duration excludes the downtime duration.

 

Wrapping Up

You can use the Data Extraction CDS views to extract PP/DS data from your SAP S/4HANA system. These Data Extraction views can be consumed by applications and frameworks that can consume data extraction CDS views (for example, SAP BW, etc.).

For detailed documentation of the views, see this SAP Help page.

Please feel free to share your feedback and comments.

P.S.: To ensure that all the prerequisites are maintained in your SAP S/4HANA system, refer the SAP Note 3562680.

entry.title: Intra-Company Purchasing in S/4 HANA: A Guide to Efficient STO Processes
entry.content:
Intra-company purchasing in SAP is a powerful solution that enables seamless stock transfers and transactions between different entities within the same organization. By leveraging SAP’s robust functionalities, businesses can ensure accurate inventory management, cost control, and streamlined logistics.

This blog dives into the key steps involved in setting up intra-company purchasing in SAP. From defining the sales organization for the supplying company code to configuring delivery types and assigning shipping points, we’ll explore the essential configurations required to optimize internal supply chains. Whether you’re creating a customer in the supplying company code, extending material master data, this guide will walk you through the process to ensure a smooth and efficient intra-company purchasing setup.

Step 1: Define Sales Organization for Company Code

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Sales and Distribution --> Define copy, delete, check sales organization.

Step 2: Define Distribution Channel

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Sales and Distribution --> Define copy, delete, check distribution channel

Step 3: Maintain sales office 

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Sales and Distribution --> Maintain sales office 

Step 4: Maintain Sales Group

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Definition --> Sales and Distribution --> Maintain Sales group

Step 5: Assign Sales Org. to Company Code

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Sales and Distribution --> Assign sales organization to Company Code

Step 6: Assign distribution channel to sales organization

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Sales and Distribution --> Assign distribution channel to sales organization 

Step 7: Assign division to sales organization

Path: SPRO --> SAP Reference IMG --> Enterprise Structure --> Assignment --> Sales and Distribution --> Assign division to sales organization

Step 8: Create Customer in Supplying Company Code (Transaction: BP)

Use BP roles FLCU00, FLCU01

Company Code Data

Sales & Distribution Data

Customer 23 created in Supplying Company Code

Step 9: Create Material Master Record (Transaction: MM01)

Maintain Sales views, General Plant/Storage data & Accounting Views.

Post creation extend the same material for receiving plant.

Material INTRACOMPANY MAT Created.

Step 10: Define Shipping Data for Plants under Set up STO

Path: SPRO --> Material Management --> Purchasing --> Purchase Order --> Set up stock transport order --> Define Shipping data for plants

Choose Supplying Plant and fill the relevant data

Choose Receiving Plant and fill the relevant data

Step 11: Configure Delivery Type

Path: SPRO --> Material Management --> Purchasing --> Purchase Order --> Set up stock transport order--> Configure Delivery Type and Availability Check Procedure by Plant.

NLCC – With billing document

NL – Without billing document

Step 12: Assign Document Type, One-Step procedure, Under delivery Tolerance

Path: SPRO --> Material Management --> Purchasing --> Purchase Order --> Set up stock transport order --> Assign Document Type, One-Step procedure, under delivery tolerance

Step 13: Assign Shipping Points in Logistics Execution

Path: SPRO --> Logistics Execution --> Shipping --> Basic Shipping Functions --> Shipping Points and Goods Receiving Point Determination --> Assign Shipping Points

Step 14: Stock Overview (Transaction: MMBE)

Step 15: Create Purchase Order (Transaction: ME21N)

PO 4500000247 Created.

Check the stock in MMBE

Step 16:  Create Outbound Delivery

Post Processing 

Get the Delivery Number

Step 17: Picking Outbound Delivery

Step 18: Manage Outbound Deliveries

Once GI posted, you can check the document process flow

Check the FI Documents

Step 19: Check In-Transit Stock (Transaction: MB5T)

Step 20: Post Goods Receipt against Outbound Delivery

Material Document 5000000312 Posted.

Step 21: Check Stock Overview in MMBE

 

Thanks & Regards,

Leelamohan Mannem

entry.title: The NEW New Service Connection Process for Energy and Water
entry.content:
New service connection, a complex business
This process is a perfect example of integrating customer service, enterprise asset management and field service. Utilities have not changed how buildings are connected to the grid, however, work management operations and software solutions have improved significantly. Let me show you how the redesigned new service connection process runs with SAP service management, both from a customer and work management view. 

Reference process flow
If you compare a water connection for a single home with an electric connection for an office building, you can see both, a variation in complexity and a common pattern. We considered this to define a reference process with SAP service management. It has two main parts, a commercial phase followed by a technical phase. The main solutions are service and maintenance functions in S/4HANA Cloud private edition and Field Service Management.

Process steps

Steps 1 - 4, commercial phase:
First, during new construction, either a land developer, builder or contractor requests a new service connection. The process starts in customer service, online or with an agent. A service request is created and captures customer information, the new service location, dates and the type of request. Next is a follow-up service order for a field survey. A technician needs to assess, measure and plan the new connection design. With this information, the service agent can create a service quote and send it to the requestor.  When the quote has been accepted, the first phase, mostly commercial and customer centric, is complete.

Steps 5 - 8, technical execution phase:
In the second phase, a work manager receives the service order, plans the execution operations, equipment, material, schedules a crew and dispatches the work. A field crew performs the installation, time and material is recorded in the field, orders are confirmed. This phase, work and asset management centric, concludes when the orders are confirmed, billed and settled. The builder is informed that the connection is ready for meter installation.

Document flow
As you can tell by now, this is a long workflow, typically taking weeks. From a utility perspective, it is an 'ad-hoc service request' by an external party. That's why the backbone of this process is service with advanced execution, where service starts with a customer request and the execution is complex and linked to asset management. See also my other blog about this topic. 

Reference document flow new service connection process

What has changed and what is special?
Compared with previous solutions and releases, for example SAP ECC work management, customer service (CS), service management (PM/CS) or mobile solutions, many capabilities have changed. Let me point them out and add important remarks.

Service request and service order
The initial object is the (new) service request, followed by a (new) service order. I say (new) service to highlight the (new) service objects that became available in S/4HANA service with customer management. The service request is either created directly in S/4HANA, via an online form or from Service Cloud. Keep in mind, we do no longer use the sunset PM/CS objects, service notification PM-CS-SN and service orders PM-CS-SO.

This service request contains a customer/sold-to-party, dates, and we need a service loaction. If the functional location does not exist yet, it is created now or at least a building address is captured. Nothing new, just note that the new service objects can reference locations and equipment.

From here on there are about 10 subsequent process documents that follow the service request. They are connected via document flows, as shown in the picture above. This is very useful, copy control configuration and standard integrations automate the process.

Following the service request, the service order for the field survey uses a simple transaction type SRVO and a simple service product. I call it simple because we do not need any plant maintenance involved yet. This order is for a field technician to visit the construction site and gather information to plan the connection design. 

Field service management
The service order is dispatched to a surveyor in the field. Mind the service order to field service management (FSM) document flow, we utilize FSM rather than SAP Service and Asset Manager (SAM). The mobile FSM app uses smartforms to capture the connection specifics, for example 'residential electric service, 240V 2-phase, 200A, 15m long, above ground'. 

Service quotation
The service quotation document is key for the commercial agreement and for preparing the work management phase. The ability to quote service, including complex service with advanced execution, is new in S/4HANA. A quotation of the document type MSQ is a precursor of the service order with advanced execution of the type MSO. A different service product is used for the connection order item, now with an item category for advanced execution.

The calculation of the new connection amount payable by the builder is happening here, using item pricing. The utility can use simple or complex rules to determine a connection price. A debit request can be created for an upfront payment. 


Screen capture of a service quotation

Once the builder accepts the quotation, the maintenance service order is created automatically and visible in the transaction history document flow.

Service order with advanced execution
The service order with advanced execution of the type MSO (maintenance service order) links us to plant maintenance. Not much has changed in the linked PM order, see below, but watch the 'Service' section. This is new, it contains the link from PM back to service.  


Screen capture of a maintenance order

The maintenance order operations drive the field service activities. 


Screen capture of a maintenance order, operations


Field service management, again
FSM takes over with planning, dispatching and the field service execution. This is the heavy lifting of the service with advanced execution. Field work can include digging a trench, installing cables or pipes, setting poles and transformers, and mounting conduits and meter sockets. Field technicians record time, material, expenses and update equipment records in the mobile app.


Screen capture of the FSM dispatching board

You may have noticed, FSM works for both, simple customer centric service orders like the survey and here, for complex plant maintenance centric operations.

Completion
Once the order is technically complete, the new service, for example power or water, can be turned on. Because most services are metered, this starts the next process, a device installation in meter-to-cash. The confirmed orders are internally settled and billed. The new connection becomes a field asset, located at a customer address.

Summary
The redesigned service process is compatible with the current SAP solutions and suitable for most use cases, especially residential and commercial connections. The benefit of the new service objects is the flexibility and customer orientation. When needed for advanced execution, we use the extensive capabilities in plant maintenance. 

There are some exceptions to the applicability of this reference process. For example, a connection for an entire factory to the grid at a high voltage level would rather be seen as a capital project and managed like an investment.

entry.title: Three new supply chain logistics customer stories
entry.content:
Q1 brought three customer stories of supply chain logistics transformation.  

Here's to our valued customers, and the dedicated teams who bring value to their customers.

Celsa UK: Enabling closer collaboration with carriers to create a future-ready supply chain network

"When steel producer Celsa UK looked to improve cost-efficiency and visibility across its logistics network, it realized that effective collaboration with its carriers would be key to success. To help it achieve this, the company implemented SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition, transportation management and the SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration solution."   (Celsa webinar)

✔️1-2% savings on transport costs | ✔️95% of road carriers collaborating through the network | ✔️83% on-time deliveries

Bondalti: Transforming logistics processes to better manage transportation, yard operations, and carrier collaboration

"A leading specialty chemicals and solutions company, Bondalti sought to reimagine its logistics processes. The SAP Transportation Management and SAP Yard Logistics applications as well as the SAP Business Network Freight Collaboration solution provide improved visibility and planning in transportation while integrating carriers for freight management and supply chain transparency."

✔️4% average reduction in transportation costs | ✔️90% of carrier invoices received digitally, compliant, and error-free | ✔️25% less time spent by trucks in the plants

LAPP: Keeping industries moving by delivering certainty in an unpredictable world with enhanced supply chain visibility

"Many of LAPP’s customers are manufacturing companies that operate on tight production schedules, so any delays in shipping can cause significant disruption. To give its customers more certainty about delivery dates, LAPP wanted to boost transparency and efficiency in its inbound supply chain through smarter collaboration with suppliers and carriers."

✔️90%+ faster automated confirmation of purchase orders | ✔️30%+ time savings on order management and delivery tracking for inbound deliveries | ✔️10% productivity gains for customer service and logistics teams

 

Learn more about #LogisticsVisibility: 
SAP Community posts
SAP Business Network Global Track and Trace: product tour
Coming soon: Supply Chain Logistics maturity model for self-assessment and business case

entry.title: What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2503
entry.content:
Here are the latest features released for SAP Asset Performance Management in 2503. We are pleased to announce significant updates, including Visual Asset Health Monitoring. This release also includes improvements to Manage Rules, Manage Alerts, and Manage Recommendations. 

Asset Health Monitoring

Enable Visual Asset Health Monitoring

SAP Asset Performance Management 2503 enables Visual Asset Health Monitoring. You can elevate your asset management strategy with AI-powered visual inspections.

By collecting and analyzing images of your assets, you can gain real-time insights into their health and predict potential failures before they occur. By documenting asset condition over time, Visual Asset Health Monitoring provides valuable insights into an asset's health and performance. Using AI-powered image analysis, it is possible to measure wear and predict potential failure with greater accuracy. Analyzing images can help identify issues before they lead to operational disruptions. This allows for timely alerts and maintenance notifications, preventing costly downtime and ensuring optimal asset performance. 
(*This is applicable for embedded IoT only)

Key benefits

  • Provides insights into asset health and performance
  • Uses AI-powered image analysis to measure wear and predict potential failure
  • Helps identify issues before they cause operational disruptions
  • Enables timely alerts and maintenance notifications
  • Prevents costly downtime and ensures optimal asset performance
  • Offers potential for improved efficiency, accuracy, and cost-effectiveness compared to manual inspections

For how to leverage Visual Asset Health Monitoring: see Visual Asset Health Monitoring | SAP Help Portal
You can also watch this video : End to end demo of Visual Inspection for Condition Monitoring.

Initiate Rule on-demand

You can now initiate a rule on-demand at any point of time, without having to wait for the automated run of the rule as per configurations. This would be helpful to perform a testing after setting a new rule or changing a rule. To do so, select the desired rule that is in Active status and click on the Run Rule Now button. This button is disabled when you select a rule that is not in active status. Read more on Triggering a rule (*This is applicable for embedded IoT only)

There are more enhancements with Rules. 

  • Manage Rule Runs API: The API is enhanced to view rule execution results, including errors, rule details, activation, execution, and involved technical objects. For more information, see Manage Rule Runs API.
  • Initiate Rule through API: To trigger a monitoring or a derivation rule only via API, you can choose the API only option in the Settings. For more information on the API, see Manage Rule Runs API in the SAP Business Acceleration Hub. Triggering a rule through the API can only be performed on-demand.

Navigate to Origin - Rule from Monitor Alerts 

Monitor Alerts in 2503 has been enhanced with a new feature that allows users to easily navigate to the Origin-Rule of alerts. Users can now click on a hyperlink associated with each alert to view the Rule that generated it. This enables quick checks of how Rules are defined and configured so that users can more efficiently investigate and understand why specific alerts are being triggered. (*This is applicable for embedded IoT only)

Asset Reliability Engineering

Configure filter options for priority type in Recommendations

Configuring priority filters in recommendations ensures that users can focus to prioritise the most important tasks, improving decision-making and efficiency. You can activate or deactivate priority types using the Recommendation Settings in the Configuration Manager application. The configured settings will then be reflected in the Manage Recommendations and Implement Recommendations applications, allowing users to filter and manage recommendations based on priority levels.

Enhanced visibility of lifecycle status in Recommendations

In asset management, understanding the lifecycle status of assigned technical objects, maintenance plans, and task lists is crucial for effective decision-making. To improve visibility, recommendations now display lifecycle statuses of these objects, ensuring that potential conflicts are identified and addressed promptly.

Warning icons for quick Identification
To make it easier to spot conflicts , warning icons have been added to both the Recommendation list page and object page. These icons appear in the following scenarios:

  • Technical Objects: If one or more assigned technical objects are with statuses deletion flag or deletion indicator.
  • Maintenance Plans: If a maintenance plan has a frequency mismatch or is in Object Deactivated, Deletion Flag, or Deletion Indicator status. 
  • Task Lists: If a task list has a status other than “Released (General)” or “Released for Order”.

Visual Cues in the Assignments Tab 
Beyond warning icons, visual cues in the Assignments tab help users quickly assess lifecycle status at a glance. By integrating these enhancements, users can efficiently manage assets, reducing the risk of overlooked issues or conflicts, ensuring that recommendations align with the current state of technical objects, maintenance plans, and task list.

  • Technical Objects flagged with a deletion flag or deletion indicator are visually highlighted.
  • Task Lists with a status other than “Released (General)” or “Release for Order”.
  • Maintenance Plans affected by a frequency mismatch, Object Deactivated, Deletion Flag, or Deletion Indicator status are distinctly marked. 

This is the release highlights I prepared for the release 2503. Hope this provides you a good overview. See below for a quick reminder how to keep in touch with us. 

Keep In Touch

  • Check out all past and present Intelligent Asset Management webcasts here. If you register for an IAM webcast, you will be automatically subscribed to our IAM Community Engagement Newsletter. 
  • Check some of our recent blogs:
    • What's New in SAP Asset Performance Management 2502 - SAP Community
    • Why Failure-Mode Focus Matters in Intelligent Asset Management 
    • SAP Asset Performance Management Embedding Cumulocity IoT to Drive Innovations in IoT and AI
    • How AI-based Anomaly Detection empowers reliability engineers in SAP Asset Performance Management
  • Subscribe to What's New on the SAP Help Portal
  • Submit or vote for the improvement you would like to see in the SAP Continuous Influence session

entry.title: The Symphony of Human-AI Collaboration
entry.content:
In the fast-growing landscape of technology, the partnership between humans and artificial intelligence is no longer a science fiction movie but a present reality reshaping the world, even including supply chain management. As supply chains become increasingly complex, geographically dispersed, and vulnerable to disruptions, the collaboration between human expertise and AI capabilities has emerged as the cornerstone of resilience and sustainability.

Beyond Digitization: The “Human-AI” Alliance

While digitization of supply chains has been underway for nearly two decades, today's challenges demand more than just digital transformation.

  • Shift of Focus: The focus has shifted toward creating adaptable, responsive systems that can withstand unforeseen shocks while maintaining operational efficiency. This is where human-AI collaboration truly shines.
  • Humans+AI: Unlike fully automated systems, collaborative approaches play to the strengths of both humans and machines. We humans bring contextual understanding, ethical judgment, creative problem-solving, and relationship skills that remain uniquely human. Meanwhile, AI offers incredible data processing capabilities, pattern recognition, predictive analytics, and consistency in repetitive tasks that would exhaust any human.

The Collaborative Advantage

The most successful supply chain operations have moved beyond seeing AI as either a job-killer or just another tool. Instead, they've cultivated a partnership where each enhances the other's capabilities. A few examples below.

  • Demand Planning: Take demand planning, for example - AI can analyze mountains of historical data, market trends, and external factors to generate remarkably accurate forecasts. But human planners add crucial insights about upcoming marketing campaigns, competitor moves, or geopolitical events that might not show up in historical patterns.
  • Risk Management: Similarly, in risk management, AI can constantly monitor thousands of potential disruption signals across global networks, but human managers provide the essential judgment to decide which risks need immediate action and how to balance competing priorities when resources are limited.

Orchestrating the Human-AI Symphony

Creating effective collaboration between humans and AI in supply chain management requires thoughtful design and orchestration across D(istribution)-E(xplainability)-L(earning)-I(nterfaces).

  1. Intentional Role Distribution
    The best implementations clearly define which tasks AI should handle, which should stay primarily human-driven, and which benefit from active collaboration. AI agents can revolutionize data processing and analyzing while humans excel in making big decisions with emotional contexts. Just like with business partners, playing to the strengths of each other while acknowledging their limitations spells for the story of a successful collaboration.
  2. Transparency and Explainability
    For us to work effectively with AI systems, we need to understand why they make certain recommendations. This transparency builds trust and helps us decide when to follow AI guidance and when to override it based on factors the AI might miss.
  3. Continuous Learning Loops
    The human-AI partnership should improve over time through two-way learning. AI systems learn from our feedback and corrections, while we develop new insights from patterns the AI identifies. This creates a virtuous cycle of continuous improvement.
  4. Adaptive Interfaces
    The touchpoints between humans and AI should facilitate seamless collaboration. This includes intuitive dashboards that present AI-generated insights in actionable formats, natural language interfaces for conversational interactions, and feedback mechanisms that let us easily guide AI systems.

Real-World Applications

The collaborative approach is already yielding impressive results across the supply chain spectrum:

  • Collaborative Planning and Execution

Modern supply chain platforms now enable a collaborative approach where AI generates initial plans based on historical data and constraints, while human planners review, adjust, and finalize these plans based on their domain expertise and market knowledge. Over time, the AI learns from these human adjustments, becoming increasingly aligned with the planners' strategic thinking.

  • Exception Management

In day-to-day operations, AI systems can monitor thousands of data points to identify potential exceptions or disruptions. Instead of overwhelming human operators with alerts, collaborative systems prioritize issues based on impact and urgency, presenting only the most critical situations for human attention along with relevant context and recommended actions.

  • Sustainability Initiatives

The pursuit of sustainability in supply chains benefits tremendously from human-AI collaboration. AI can optimize routes for fuel efficiency, predict maintenance needs to extend equipment life cycles, and identify opportunities for waste reduction. Human stakeholders provide the values framework that guides these optimizations, ensuring that improvements align with broader environmental and social goals beyond mere cost reduction.

Challenges and Considerations

Despite its promise, effective human-AI collaboration in supply chains faces several challenges:

  1. Skill Development
    Supply chain professionals need new skills to work effectively with AI systems, including data literacy, critical thinking about algorithmic outputs, and the ability to translate business context into parameters that AI can understand.
  2. Trust Calibration
    We must develop appropriate levels of trust in AI systems—neither blindly following recommendations nor dismissing them outright. This requires experience with the systems' capabilities and limitations.
  3. Ethical Frameworks
    As AI takes on more decision-making responsibility, we must ensure that ethical considerations around fairness, sustainability, and labour impacts are properly incorporated into AI systems and processes.

The Path Forward

The future of supply chain management isn't about choosing between human expertise and AI - it's about thoughtfully orchestrating their collaboration. Organizations that master this partnership will build supply chains that are not just more efficient but also more resilient, innovative, and aligned with broader societal goals.

Moving forward, the most successful supply chain leaders will be those who view AI not as a technology implementation but as a collaborative partner in an ongoing journey of improvement and adaptation. The symphony of human-AI collaboration, when properly conducted, creates harmonies that neither could achieve alone - resulting in supply chains that truly exceed the sum of their parts!

What is SAP doing?

At SAP, we are exactly trying to create these symphonies in our Supply Chain solutions portfolio.

  • Building on a Strong Foundation: SAP's market-leading Business AI platform lays the groundwork for innovative applications of narrow, generative, and agentic AI. Through strategic collaborations with top AI platforms like Databricks, AlephAlpha, Anthropic, Google Cloud, and IBM, SAP uniquely enables businesses to harness the power of AI at scale. Additionally, SAP is committed to upskilling its own employees in AI, ensuring they remain at the forefront of this transformative technology
  • Building Trust through AI: SAP is integrating AI-driven innovations into its Supply Chain solutions, including Integrated Business Planning (IBP), Field Services Management (FSM), Integrated Product Development (IPD). These enhancements provide R&D engineers, supply chain planners, and service managers with accurate and dependable support, empowering them to make informed decisions with confidence.
  • Accelerating Innovation through Partnership: Our partners play a vital role in delivering AI innovations to our customers. Moreover, leveraging their extensive industry and functional expertise, many supply chain management partners are developing tailored AI solutions for our shared customers, driving further value and success.

In a nutshell, SAP, together with its partners, is poised to propel supply chain leaders into the future with the latest innovations in AI.

What did you think about this post? Please leave your feedback, thoughts, and suggestions in a comment below.

Also, please follow the co-authors: @sarvagya_s and @pavneetbedi  so that you do not miss any future posts.

You may also be interested in the following blogs:

    • Transforming Digital Supply Chains with AI
    • Unlock your supply chain potential with SAP Integrated Business Planning AI
    • When it comes to leveraging AI in Supply Chain – The future is NOW!
    • The Secret of Santa’s Supply Chain

entry.title: SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration 2503 Release - What's New?
entry.content:
On March 16th 2025, we released exciting new SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration innovations for our customers.

Let me share some of the key highlights from the SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration 2503 Release.

Cloud-based controlling and triggering of ERP integration

Scope:

  • User ability to trigger ERP integration from SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration through an event and the corresponding UI
  • Before replicating or synchronizing a technical object such as equipment or functional location from SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration to the ERP system, user ability to:
    • Select the specific ERP system to be considered for integration or synchronization
    • Change the category type of the object or standard fields and attributes

Key Benefits:

  • Leverage additional control on the objects and fields to be replicated or synchronized to the ERP system
  • Select the ERP system to be considered for replication or synchronization from the cloud
  • Enhance end-to-end integration scenarios through a dedicated event for ERP replication or synchronization

Description:

Before the SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration 2503 Release, when a technical objects (i.e. equipment or functional location) was created or received from an external organization, the replication or mapping of this object to the connected SAP ERP system was automatically triggered by the corresponding created or shared event respectively. In the case of a received technical object, this event was propagated to all connected SAP ERP systems. Based on the settings maintained in the Mobile Add-on, the technical object was then automatically approved, rejected or subjected to a review workflow in each SAP ERP system. the picture below shows this behavior.

 

With the SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration 2503 Release a dedicated event to trigger the replication or mapping of newly created or received technical objects has been introduced (see picture below).

To enable this functionality, the SAP Note 3500076 must be deployed.

Whit this new functionality, the user can create or receive a technical object and review and update header data, ERP-specific data, technical specification and documentation. When done, the user can then decide to replicate or map this technical objects and all relevant dependent data to one specific SAP ERP system by clicking on the ERP Sync button in the ERP information section of the technical object.

The synchronization to SAP ERP can be also triggered by the corresponding APIs for equipment and functional locations. 

By this innovation, the user has full control on technical object data before replicating or mapping the object and dependent data to the backend SAP ERP system. This might massively improved the standardization, completeness, consistency and quality of asset records in SAP ERP. In addition, there is no need to maintain settings in the Mobile Add-on the accept, reject or review objects being synchronized by SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration.

External numbering for equipment

Scope:

  • Replication of equipment from SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration to SAP ERP, supporting external numbering for equipment in SAP ERP

Key Benefits:

  • Enhance integration between SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration and SAP ERP

Description:

When you create equipment in SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration and synchronizes it with the SAP ERP system, the ERP system automatically generates an ID. This ID links as the External ID of the equipment in SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration.

With the 2503 release, you can create equipment with an external number range in ERP during the synchronization from SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration to ERP. To enable this feature, implement SAP Note 3500076.

 

Inbound integration of shared functional locations

Scope:

  • Enabling inbound integration of the shared functional location so that operator and service provider can sync the shared functional location to their SAP ERP system

Key Benefits:

  • Enhance integration between SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration and SAP ERP

Description:

With 2503 release, inbound synchronization of shared functional locations is supported. For a shared functional location, you can select the SAP ERP system, structure indicator, and functional location category. You can also maintain the Proposed ERP ID based on the selected structure indicator and synchronizes it to ERP. To enable this feature, implement SAP Note 3500076.

Automatic unsharing of equipment and functional locations

Scope:

  • When an automatic sharing pattern is activated, ability to automatically unshare an equipment or functional location by removing the external trading partner assigned to the equipment or functional location

Key Benefits:

  • Enhance the management of shared equipment and functional location across their lifecycle

Description:

This feature enables you to automatically unshare their equipment and functional locations with your business partners.

Currently, equipment or a functional location is automatically shared upon publishing. This sharing extends to all business partners associated with that object, provided that sharing patterns is set for those business partners.

You now have the option to automatically unshare objects using the Automatic Unsharing option.

Other Innovations

  • Document ID-Based Filtering in Search APIs (Equipment, Models and Functional Locations search API). In the search APIs, the response can now include list of document IDs assigned to the object as well based on the request payload. This enables document ID-based filtering in search APIs.
  • Mass Upload Models with Public Image URLs. The new feature allows you to include public URLs of image files in the Image column of the Microsoft Excel template during a mass upload of models. This functionality streamlines the process by automatically downloading and uploading images as model logos.

Upcoming Innovations

Following exciting innovations are planned for the upcoming major release of SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration:

  • Attribute-based search for Documents
  • Replication of SAP ERP Materials to SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration Models
  • Assignment of components to work orders
  • Templates for notifications
  • Etc.

Submit innovation requests

We encourage you to visit the SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration Customer Influence Portal to submit your innovation requests and review and vote innovation requests submitted by other customers.

entry.title: Enhancing Your SAP Field Service Management Experience with Continuous Quality Checks
entry.content:
Are you implementing SAP Field Service Management (FSM) or managing its day-to-day operations? In both scenarios, ensuring the quality and stability of your solution is vital. SAP Enterprise Support offers continuous quality checks to help you achieve this. Continuous quality checks are included as part of your SAP Enterprise Support cloud edition maintenance contract, embedded in your field service management solutions subscription at no extra cost.


SAP Field Service Management supports your technicians with mobile tools, accelerating issue resolution and making the service experience effortless for customers. As an AI cloud enabled solution, FSM contributes to increased customer satisfaction through proactive service features.

Below is a list of continuous quality checks (CQCs) available to support your FSM solution, tailored to your specific implementation phase and needs:

 

ServiceBenefitWhen to request
CQC for Deployment Readiness for SAP Field Service Management

Ensures operational efficiency and readiness by performing a detailed analysis of your FSM, either before go-live or as a "health check" for your productive systems.

(Highly recommended) 
Realize phase - Two months prior to go live 

(Proactive) Run phase 

 

Additional Resources and Support:

This blog post* offers a detailed information about CQC for Deployment Readiness for FSM, including guidance on how to request them.

For more information on other CQC service offerings for supply chain solutions, visit the Continuous Quality Checks* section of our Supply Chain Management Solutions value map*.

*To access the link and for ongoing support and actionable guidance tailored to your unique SAP journey, we invite you to join and explore SAP Enterprise Support Value Maps. 

Enhance Your SAP Experience with Value Maps 

Beyond CQC services, SAP Enterprise Support offers additional resources to help you drive innovation. SAP Enterprise Support value maps provide expert-led resources, tailored recommendations, and advisory services - all included in your SAP cloud subscription. Get started today: 

  • One-time sign up for SAP Learning Hub, edition for SAP Enterprise Support, using a valid S-User ID (please allow up to 2 hours for processing) 
  • Access SAP Enterprise Support value maps for personalized guidance 
  • Request a call with our solution area experts for tailored advice 

Value Maps are part of SAP Enterprise Support commitment to your success. Visit the SAP Enterprise Support value maps landing page to explore our range of topics and start engaging with us today. 

 

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

About the author: 

  • Mabelle Roque Miguel is a Focus Advisor for SAP Enterprise Support value maps for SAP Supply Chain Management solutions. Based in Singapore, Mabelle has been with SAP since 2007, taking on diverse roles that have largely centered around customer engagement and success. Now, her focus is on guiding businesses through their supply chain journey, helping them accelerate the adoption of SAP supply chain management solutions with value map content and SAP Enterprise Support offerings.

Have questions, comments or feedback? Don't hesitate to comment down below or contact @Mabelle.

entry.title: SAP EWM: Wave Management (Configuration & Execution)
entry.content:
What is SAP EWM Wave Management?

Wave Management in SAP EWM is a process that allows warehouses to group and manage picking tasks based on specific criteria such as order priority, product availability, and resource allocation. By organizing pick tasks into waves, warehouses can optimize labor resources, improve order fulfillment times, and enhance overall operational efficiency.

Key Benefits of Wave Management

  1. Improved Efficiency: Grouping orders into waves allows for more efficient picking processes and reduces travel time within the warehouse.
  2. Enhanced Visibility: Wave Management provides real-time visibility into order processing, allowing managers to monitor progress and make informed decisions.
  3. Optimized Resource Allocation: By analyzing wave data, organizations can allocate labor and equipment resources more effectively.
  4. Increased Order Accuracy: Wave picking helps reduce errors by enabling pickers to focus on a specific group of orders, minimizing distractions.

Configuration of SAP EWM Wave Management

Define Warehouse Process Type

You can create a separate warehouse process type to manage wave as I have created Warehouse Process Type “OBWA ” for this example.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Warehouse Process Type

Select process category “2” as we are creating this process type for stock removal.

Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

Define determination rule for new created OBWA Warehouse Process Type and Control Indicator “04” for Outbound deliveries.

Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

For new Warehouse Process Type OBWA, configured to determine Storage Type Search Sequence based on Stock Removal Control + Warehouse Process Type + Stock Type Search Sequence + Stock Removal Rule.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Strategies > Determine Storage Type Search Sequence for Stock Removal

Maintain Wave Types

Create wave types according to your requirements, for this example I have created two wave types

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Maintain Wave Types

Maintain Wave Categories

Create wave categories according to your requirements, for this example I have created two wave categories

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Maintain Wave Categories

Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type

Select “Auto Wave Creation” check for Warehouse Process Type OBWA

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > General Settings > Set Automatic Wave Generation for Warehouse Process Type

Define Condition Tables

Define condition table for wave configuration including the fields that you are planning to use for waves, for this example I have created table ZN81SHIP and included WME_CUSTOMER, WME_LGNUM, WME_PROCTY fields

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination >Define Condition Tables

Define Access Sequences

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Access Sequences

Define Condition Types

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Condition Types

Define Determination Procedure

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Define Determination Procedure

Assign Procedure to Document Type

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Issue Process > Wave Management > Wave Template Determination > Assign Procedure to Document Type

Define Condition Maintenance Group

Add new created condition type and tables in “WME” Maintenance Groups with Application “WME”, and Usage “WD”

Maintain Wave Templates

Use transaction /SCWM/WAVETMP to create Wave Template(s) using new created wave types & wave categories for Manual & Automatic waves as per your requirements.

Make sure you maintain at least below time attributes for cutoff & release time: Cutoff Time, Release Time, WaveCompletion, and Wave Calendar

Wave Determination Group Condition Maintenance

Use transaction /SCWM/WDGCM to maintain conditions for wave determination

Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Defined Sort Rule “PIPA” and assigned Sort Fields “PATHSEQ” Sort Sequence

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Sort Rules for Warehouse Tasks

Define Filters for Warehouse Order Creation Rules

Created 3 Filters

FLT1 — Weight between 1- 10 LB for Wave Cat C1
FLT2 — Weight upto 10 LB for Wave Cat C2
FLT3 — Weight between 11- 100 kg for Wave Cat C2

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Filters for Warehouse Order Creation Rules

Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Defined below limits:
LMT1 Max 1 task in one WO
LMT2 Max 5 task in one WO

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Limit Values for the Size of a Warehouse Order

Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

Create profile(s) and assign your Sort Rule “PIPA”, and select auto HU & WT creation

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Packing Profile for Warehouse Order Creation

Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Create WOCR and link with new created Filter, Limit, Sorting and Packaging Profile

WOC1 WOCR — Outbound — 1
WOC2 WOCR — Outbound — 2
WOC3 WOCR — Outbound — 3

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Creation Rules for Warehouse Orders

Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Created Sort Sequence for new WOCR & Activity Area T210

N810 T210 PICK 1 WOC1 WOCR — Outbound — 1
N810 T210 PICK 2 WOC2 WOCR — Outbound — 2
N810 T210 PICK 3 WOC3 WOCR — Outbound — 3

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Order > Define Search Sequence of Creation Rules for Activity Areas

Define Warehouse Process Type

Created a Warehouse Process Type “PIHU” for Picking HU

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Warehouse Process Type

Define Warehouse Number Control

Added PIHU in Warehouse Number Control under field “Warehouse Process Type for Pick-HU WT for Current Resource” and selected Pick-HU Procedure as “0BDL”

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Warehouse Task > Define Control Indicators for Determining Warehouse Process Types

Assign this Control Indicator to your product

Create PackSpec 

Created new PackSpec for Outbound Wave

Define Activity Areas

Created new general purpose activity area for Storage Type we rare planning to use

Assign Storage Bins to Activity Areas

Define Sort Sequence for Activity Areas

Create virtual bin for activity 

Use transaction /SCWM/LS01 to create new Bin VIRTUAL_PACKBIN_01 in your storage type T210

Maintain Additional Attributes for activity area

Use transaction /SCWM/SEBA and map new Bin VIRTUAL_PACKBIN_01 to Pick Activity in Activity Area T210

Create Sales Order

Create Outbound Delivery

Verify in monitor that Wave is created according to the configurations and new outbound delivery is assigned to new created wave

Also can go to PPF actions of Outbound Delivery and check logs tab under Action Details for step by step logs

Simulate the wave release

From Monitor Outbound > Waves select wave and click Simulate Single wave release from actions button menu to review warehouse tasks and steps that will be created after it’s release

Release the Wave

You can release wave from simulate screen or from wave action toolbar button. 

Once wave is released warehouse task gets created according to the configurations we made.

Monitor and Analyze Waves

Monitoring wave performance is crucial for ongoing optimization. SAP EWM provides various tools for tracking wave progress and analyzing performance metrics.

Conclusion

SAP EWM Wave Management is a powerful tool that can significantly enhance warehouse efficiency and order fulfillment accuracy. By following the steps outlined in this guide, organizations can configure and implement wave management effectively, leading to improved operational performance.

Wave Management allows businesses to adapt to changing demands and optimize their resources, ensuring that they remain competitive in today’s fast-paced environment. As companies continue to evolve and grow, mastering Wave Management in SAP EWM will be essential for achieving operational excellence and meeting customer expectations.

In summary, by leveraging the capabilities of SAP EWM Wave Management, organizations can streamline their warehouse operations, reduce costs, and ultimately deliver better service to their customers.

entry.title: SAP EWM: Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type
entry.content:
SAP EWM: Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type

In the realm of warehouse management, effective storage strategies are vital for maximizing space utilization, enhancing operational efficiency, and improving inventory accuracy. SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) offers a robust framework for managing various storage strategies, one of which is the Pallet Storage Strategy by Handling Unit (HU) Type. This article delves into the intricacies of this storage strategy, detailing end-to-end configurations and best practices for implementation.

Understanding Pallet Storage Strategy by Handling Unit Type

What is a Handling Unit?

In SAP EWM, a Handling Unit (HU) refers to a physical unit of goods that can be managed as a single entity. This could be a pallet, carton, or any other container that holds items. The Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type allows warehouses to define how these handling units are stored based on their characteristics, such as size, weight, or type of goods.

Why Use a Pallet Storage Strategy?

Implementing a Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type enables organizations to:

  • Maximize Space Utilization: Different handling units can be stored according to their specific requirements, optimizing the use of available space.
  • Enhance Picking Efficiency: By defining storage strategies based on HU types, warehouses can streamline picking processes, reducing travel time for workers.
  • Improve Inventory Accuracy: Properly managed handling units minimize errors in stock management, leading to better inventory control.

End-to-End Configuration of SAP EWM Pallet Storage Strategy by HU Type

Define Storage Types

For this example created new storage type T070 for Pallet Storage. Selected Storage Behavior as“1”, HU Requirement mandatory, additional checks needs to configured HU type check , Put away rule — 5 (Empty bin value). You may also refer SAP standard storage type 0070 for basic configurations.

Define Bin Sections

Created below 2 type of bins sections for small and large types.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Putaway Rules > Storage Behavior: Pallets > Define Bin Sections

Perform Bin Sectioning for Bin Type and HU Type

Mapped existing HU types with new bin sections we created as required.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Putaway Rules > Storage Behavior: Pallets > Perform Bin Sectioning for Bin Type and HU Type

Assign Storage bin types to storage types

Assign created Bin Types to storage type T070 we created for this example.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Bin Determination > Assign Storage bin types to storage types

Define HU Types for each storage type

Assign all our HU types to storage type T070 we created for this example

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Influencing Parameters > HU Types > Define HU Types for Each Storage Type

Define HU Types for each storage bin type

Map your HU types to storage type T070 and bin types we created.

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Bin Determination > HU Types > Define HU Types for Each Storage Bin Type

Generate Activity Area from Storage Type

Generated for warehouse N810 & T070 that we created for this example

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Master Data > Activity Areas > Generate Activity Area from Storage Type

Define Storage Bin Structure 

Defined Storage Bin Structure (for ASL- Aisle/Stack/Level) as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Master Data > Storage Bins > Define Storage Bin Structure

Create Bins

Created 20 bins as per above structure and then using /SCWM/LS11 edited 10 bins for Type = SMAL

Perform Bin Sorting

Sorted bins using T-code /SCWM/SBST

Define Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Configured Storage Type Search sequence for putaway in Storage Type T070

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Master Data > Storage Type Sequences > Define Storage Type Search Sequence for Putaway

Assign storage types to storage type search sequence

Assigned search sequence to storage type as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Assign Storage Types to Storage Type Search Sequence

Define putaway control indicator

Defined putaway control P070 for new search sequence. (Use this in your product)

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Slotting > Master Data > Define Putaway Control Indicator

Specify Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Mapped Storage Type Search sequence as below

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Specify Storage Type Search sequence for putaway

Optimize access strategy for storage type search: putaway

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Goods Receipt Process > Strategies > Storage Type Search > Optimize Access Strategy for Storage Type Search: Putaway

Test your configurations

To test this process created a purchase order for product that has our new putaway control P070 assigned and inbound delivery with pallet HUs. Created HU tasks for our distributed delivery and reviewed the destination bins in our warehouse tasks. As you can notice in below image that Pallet Storage Sections are used according to the configurations we made in this example.

Conclusion

Implementing a pallet storage strategy by HU type in SAP EWM is essential for optimizing warehouse operations. By configuring handling unit types, storage types, and storage strategies, organizations can significantly enhance inventory management efficiency. This approach not only maximizes space utilization but also improves the accuracy and speed of warehouse processes. As businesses continue to evolve and face new challenges in supply chain management, leveraging advanced functionalities like HU-based storage strategies will be key to maintaining competitive advantages in a dynamic market. Proper planning, configuration, and testing are critical to the successful deployment of these strategies, ultimately leading to smoother operations and improved customer satisfaction.

entry.title: Exception Handling in SAP EWM
entry.content:
One of the critical aspects of SAP EWM system is its ability to handle exceptions. Exception handling in SAP EWM allows businesses to manage unexpected situations efficiently and ensure smooth warehouse operations. In this blog, we will explore how SAP EWM handles exceptions, the types of exceptions you may encounter, and the essential steps to configure and manage them effectively.

What are Exceptions & Exception Codes in SAP EWM?

In SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM), “exceptions” refer to situations that deviate from the normal warehouse operations, like damaged goods, incorrect quantities, or system errors, which are managed and resolved through a system of exception codes, allowing warehouse staff to handle these inconsistencies during processes like putaway or picking and update the system accordingly.

Steps to Configure and Handle Exceptions in SAP EWM

As we know SAP EWM Exception Handling works based on Business Context and Exception Step in combination of configured Exception Code profile and Internal Process code

For this example we will consider one inbound scenario where during putaway process you need to use different bin due to some issue with existing bin in the warehouse task. We will not be customizing standard exception codes, and will use standard CHBD exception code here.

Use below IMG path to check standard putaway Business Context “TPT” configuration

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Exception Handling > Maintain Business Context for Exception Codes

Ensure Operation/Execution Step “04” for RF HU Whse task Action is assigned to Business Context “TPT”

In this example we will be using Internal Process Code CHBD so check it is assigned to Business Context “TPT” & Execution Step “04”

Use below IMG path to ensure Exception Code CHBD that we are planning to use is configured for your warehouse

SPRO > SCM Extended Warehouse Management > Extended Warehouse Management > Cross-Process Settings > Exception Handling > Define Exception Codes

And assigned to assigned to Business Context “TPT” & Execution Step “04” for our warehouse

Test your exception handling configurations

For this example created a Purchase Order and Inbound Delivery 180018139

Created HU Warehouse Tasks to create test data to test our exception handling configuration as you may have noticed this warehouse order has destination bin “IOWA-01–01–05” and we will use different destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” using the Exception Code CHBD

Login to RF UI using transaction /SCWM/RFUI and navigate to Putaway by WO screen and enter above warehouse Order

Enter different destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” and press enter => Enter Exception Code CHBD on error screen => Re-enter destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” => and finally confirm the destination bin on last screen to confirm the warehouse order.

Now go back to your warehouse order and verify the new destination bin “IOWA-01–01–07” is updated and Exception Code CHBD is also reflecting as expected.

Conclusion

Effective exception handling in SAP EWM is vital for maintaining the integrity of warehouse operations and ensuring customer satisfaction. By implementing structured configurations such as defining exception types, setting handling rules, and integrating processes, organizations can proactively manage disruptions and minimize their impact on operations. The ability to monitor and report on exceptions not only aids in immediate resolution but also supports continuous improvement efforts. As supply chain complexities grow, mastering exception handling in SAP EWM will empower businesses to navigate challenges with agility and resilience, fostering a culture of operational excellence.

entry.title: Breakdowns Don’t Hurt — Surprises Do. Why Smarter MRO Planning Matters Now More Than Ever
entry.content:
Breakdowns Don’t Hurt — Surprises Do.
Why Smarter MRO Planning Matters Now More Than Ever

Tariffs. Delays. Stockouts. With new U.S. tariff hikes back on the table, SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) for MRO is the one tool that helps you stay ahead — before disruptions hit your budget.

This message couldn’t be more timely. With rising global trade tensions and renewed tariffs — particularly from the U.S. on imports from China — MRO supply chains are once again under pressure. And this isn’t just about new equipment. These changes directly impact the availability of spare parts and the maintenance services that keep critical operations running.

As The Guardian put it: “Volatile freight costs and congestion are now the norm.” So how do you plan maintenance when rules, costs, and lead times keep changing?

 

The Spreadsheet Trap: Why Old Tools Fail in Today’s MRO Supply Chain

Spreadsheets and disconnected systems might feel safe — until a sudden tariff hits. These outdated tools are slow, prone to error, and can’t handle real-time change — like a 50% spike in part costs. Without live insights, you risk overstocking (tying up cash) or running out (halting production). Maintenance, procurement, and logistics teams often operate in silos — guessing rather than planning.

The result? Avoidable downtime that drains profits. Old tools are like navigating a storm with a broken compass — they leave you stranded.

A Game-Changing Solution

SAP Integrated Business Planning (SAP IBP) for MRO is a cloud-based planning solution tailored for maintenance and operations teams. It connects seamlessly to your ERP system (e.g., SAP S/4HANA or SAP ECC), bringing together real-time data from maintenance, inventory, procurement, and suppliers.

SAP IBP MRO Integration with Source ECC or S/4 ERP

Think of it as your Intelligent Planning Cockpit — powered by AI — to help forecast needs, simulate risks, and optimise MRO spend with a single, unified view.

What You Can Do With SAP IBP for MRO: 3 Real-World Use Cases

1. Spare Parts Forecasting Based on Equipment Risk

Pain: Frequent unplanned breakdowns and stockouts of critical parts

Use AI to analyse usage history, failure trends, and asset risk profiles to forecast spare parts demand — without needing maintenance orders. Anticipate early and align sourcing across locations.

Business Impact: Fewer stockouts and emergency orders.

2. Balance Inventory Across Sites to Free Up Capital

Pain: Overstocked warehouses in one region, shortages in another

Leverage multi-echelon inventory optimisation to set ideal stock levels and reorder points across all sites. Rebalance parts before issuing new purchase orders.

Business Impact: Reduced working capital and better use of available stock.

3. Run Cost Simulations to Mitigate Tariff and Supply Risks

Pain: Sudden tariffs or shipping delays disrupting budgets

Use ‘what-if’ simulations to understand the cost and impact of external shocks — like tariffs or supplier changes. Explore alternate vendors, delivery routes, or stock strategies. Get live updates from suppliers via SAP Business Network.

Business Impact: Less financial risk, more plan stability.

Why It Matters

This isn’t just about avoiding disruption — it’s about staying ahead. SAP IBP for MRO connects the dots between maintenance, procurement, and suppliers. It gives you AI-powered foresight, flexibility, and global scalability to act — before surprises cost you.

Ready for What’s Next: AI-Powered, Risk-Aware MRO Planning

SAP IBP for MRO continues to evolve with intelligent capabilities that:

  • Detect early signs of supply chain or geopolitical risks
  • Model cost or lead time shifts in advance
  • Synchronise budgets, maintenance plans, and supplier strategies
It’s more than a planning tool — it’s your risk radar, your budget optimiser, and your supply chain co-pilot.

Resources

  • 📚SAP IBP for MRO Product Page »
  • 💡SAP IBP Community »
  • 🎓SAP IBP Learning Journey »
  • 🔴See it Live at Mastering SAP Gold Coast 2025 »

If you’re serious about resilient operations, smarter sourcing, and avoiding the next big surprise — now’s the time to act.

With SAP IBP for MRO, you don’t just plan for tomorrow. You control it.

entry.title: How to get Best Practice Content for SAP Transportation Management
entry.content:
Dear SAP TM community,

if you wanted to set up some best practice content for SAP TM, in the past SAP had provided 2970208 - SAP Best Practices for S/4HANA Supply Chain for Transportation Management 1909. This KBA contained references to Best Practices content for Inbound and Outbound Transportation as well as Stock Transport Orders and a combination of Outbound and Inbound.
